Session: AI Agents in Consulting - Practical Applications
Date: 2026-03-15
Participants: Sarah Chen, Marcus Webb, Priya Patel

Sarah: Right, let's get into it. We've been exploring how AI agents can reshape consulting delivery, specifically within the due diligence workflow. Marcus, you ran the pilot last month. What did you find?

Marcus: The results were encouraging but nuanced. We deployed an agentic system to handle the initial document review phase of a mid-market acquisition. It processed roughly 400 documents in about two hours, which would normally take a junior analyst three to four days.

Priya: That's impressive on speed, but what about accuracy? We've seen plenty of demos that look good on throughput but miss critical clauses.

Marcus: Fair point. We ran a blind comparison against the manual review. The agent caught 94% of the material findings the human team identified. It also flagged twelve additional items the team had missed, three of which were genuinely material.

Sarah: So it's not just faster, it's potentially more thorough on certain dimensions. That aligns with what Deloitte published in their January report on AI-augmented due diligence. They saw similar patterns with their internal tooling.

Priya: I want to push back slightly. The 6% miss rate concerns me. In due diligence, a single missed liability clause can invalidate the entire economic model. We need to think about this as an augmentation layer, not a replacement.

Marcus: Completely agree. The architecture we're proposing uses the agent for first-pass extraction and flagging, with a senior analyst doing the verification pass. It changes the analyst's role from reader to reviewer.

Sarah: That's a good framing. Let's talk about the commercial model. If we're reducing the junior analyst hours by 60-70%, how do we price this?

Priya: We've been modelling a value-based pricing approach. Instead of billing hours, we price on deal complexity and turnaround time. Our analysis shows we can offer a 30% fee reduction to clients while improving our margins by 15%.

Marcus: We should also consider the competitive dynamics. McKinsey Digital has already announced a similar offering. Bain is reportedly building one. If we don't move on this within Q2, we lose first-mover advantage in our segment.

Sarah: Agreed. Action items then. Marcus, can you document the pilot findings in a formal write-up by end of next week? Priya, let's get the pricing model reviewed by finance before the April board meeting. I'll set up a session with the legal team to address the liability questions around AI-assisted due diligence.

Priya: I'll also reach out to our contact at Anthropic to discuss enterprise deployment patterns. They've been helpful on the guardrails front.

Marcus: One more thing. We should capture the evaluation framework we used in the pilot. It could become a reusable asset for future AI capability assessments.

Sarah: Good call. Let's wrap there.
